title: Matthew 24
subtitle: Temple Destruction Predicted; Signs of the End; Abomination; Son of Man Coming; Be Ready

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Destruction of Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — The Temple’s Destruction; Beginning of Birth Pains

heb.paragraph: The disciples ask about the 'end of the age' (synteleia tou aiōnos) — not the end of the world (kosmos) but the consummation of the present era. The distinction matters: Jesus speaks of transition between ages, not cosmic annihilation. The present age ends; a new age begins.

Wars and disasters are 'the beginning of birth pains' (ōdinōn). The metaphor is precise: birth pains are intensely painful but purposeful — they produce new life. The suffering preceding the kingdom's arrival is not meaningless chaos but the travail of a new world being born.

tl.name: Olivet Discourse — this passage
tl.text: "Not one stone here will be left on another; every one will be thrown down." Fulfilled in AD 70 when Titus destroys Jerusalem and the Temple. The specific prediction grounds the eschatological discourse in verifiable historical fulfilment.

tl.name: Jerusalem and Temple destroyed by Rome

poi.name: Mount of Olives
poi.coords: East of Jerusalem; overlooking the Temple
poi.text: Jesus and the disciples sit on the Mount of Olives opposite the Temple. The Olivet Discourse is delivered with the Temple visible across the valley — "Do you see all these things?" The mountain's elevation and its view of the Temple grounds the eschatological discourse in a specific visible reality.

refs.note: The abomination of desolation -- Daniel's phrase applied to Jerusalem's coming desecration.
Gospel preached to all nations (v.14) -- connecting eschatological promise directly to the Great Commission. Mission and eschatology are inseparable.

notes.note: MacArthur: The disciples' admiring comments about the Temple provoke Jesus's devastating prediction: not one stone will be left on another. The Temple, destroyed in AD 70, was the visible symbol of the Mosaic covenant order. The discourse that follows addresses both the near event (Jerusalem's fall) and the far event (the end of the age).

MacArthur: The Abomination of Desolation, cited from Daniel, marks the discourse's turning point. When you see it, flee immediately -- no time to retrieve possessions. The urgency of the flight instruction implies a sudden, unmistakable, undeniable event. The false messiahs who appear in specific places (the desert, inner rooms) contrast with the Son of Man's coming, which will be as unmistakable as lightning.

Calvin on the birth pangs (odinon, v.8): the wars, famines, earthquakes, and persecutions are the beginning of birth pangs -- distressing but productive. The suffering of the church age is not meaningless; it is the pain that precedes the new creation. Those who endure to the end (v.13) will be saved -- the endurance is the evidence of genuine election, not the condition of earning salvation.

Calvin on 'no one knows the day or the hour' (v.36): not the angels, not even the Son in his human nature during his earthly ministry. The Father's timing is not revealed to any creature. Speculation about the date of the parousia is forbidden -- not from lack of curiosity but because God has deliberately withheld it to keep us in constant readiness.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the Abomination of Desolation surveys three interpretive approaches: (1) already fulfilled in AD 70 (preterist); (2) two-stage fulfilment (AD 70 and future end-time); (3) exclusively future (dispensationalist). The note concludes that Matthew's 'let the reader understand' implies a fulfilment the reader should be able to identify -- supporting AD 70 as the primary referent.

The NET note on 'this generation will certainly not pass away until all these things have happened' discusses the most debated verse in the discourse. The note surveys the major options: (1) the contemporary generation (AD 70 fulfilment); (2) the generation alive at the end; (3) genea meaning race (Jewish people will survive). The natural meaning (contemporary generation) supports the AD 70 interpretation.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson on the Olivet Discourse's opening: the disciples' admiring comment about the Temple buildings occasions Jesus's devastating prediction -- not one stone on another (v.2). On the four questions of v.3 (when? what sign? end of age?): Robertson notes the disciples conflate the Temple's destruction with the end of the age; Jesus's answer addresses both within a complex double-reference structure.

Robertson on the Abomination of Desolation (to bdelygma tes eremoseos, v.15): the phrase from Dan 9:27; 11:31; 12:11. Robertson notes 'let the reader understand' (v.15b) as the narrator's parenthetical -- the reader, not the disciples, is the audience for this aside. The interpretive options: Antiochus Epiphanes (167 BC), the Roman standards in the temple court (AD 70), or a future end-time event.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom notes the disciples' conflation of the two questions -- Temple destruction and end of the age -- as the structural ambiguity Jesus's answer navigates. The signs of vv.4-14 are both the conditions of the Jewish War and the conditions of the entire church age.

The Catena traces the patristic reading of the Son of Man coming on the clouds (v.30, citing Dan 7:13) as the parousia. Chrysostom notes the distinction between the gradual signs that precede (vv.4-28) and the sudden, unmistakable coming (v.27: like lightning). The sudden coming requires no sign to identify it -- unlike the gradual developments that the disciples must interpret.

hist.context: The Olivet Discourse (chs.24-25) is Matthew's fifth and final discourse. The first half addresses signs preceding the end: birth pains (wars, famines, persecution), the abomination, the great distress, and false messiahs. The discourse interweaves near fulfilment (Jerusalem, AD 70) and far fulfilment (the Son of Man's return) simultaneously.

sections.header: Verses 9–28 — Persecution, Apostasy, the Abomination; False Messiahs

heb.paragraph: From Daniel 9:27, 11:31, 12:11 — bdelygma is something that causes horror and disgust; erēmōsis is desolation, making a place uninhabitable. Historically fulfilled in 167 BC (Antiochus IV) and AD 70 (Roman destruction). Jesus applies it to a still-future desecration that signals the need for immediate flight.

Jesus warns of pseudochristoi — counterfeit messiahs. The prefix pseudo- does not mean 'obviously fake' but 'convincingly imitative.' The danger is not absurd claims but plausible ones. The true Christ's return will be unmistakable (like lightning, v.27), requiring no investigation.

tl.name: The Son of Man's coming — this passage
tl.text: "About that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father." The discourse refuses all date-setting. The response to uncertainty is not calculation but faithfulness: keep watch, be ready.

tl.name: The final gathering of the elect

poi.name: Mount of Olives
poi.coords: East of Jerusalem; overlooking the Temple
poi.text: Jesus and the disciples sit on the Mount of Olives opposite the Temple. The Olivet Discourse is delivered with the Temple visible across the valley — "Do you see all these things?" The mountain's elevation and its view of the Temple grounds the eschatological discourse in a specific visible reality.

refs.note: Son of Man coming on the clouds -- the Danielic vision applied to the final parousia.
As in the days of Noah -- ordinary life until sudden catastrophe. The warning is about surprise.

notes.note: MacArthur: The coming of the Son of Man on the clouds (v.30, citing Dan 7:13) is accompanied by cosmic signs -- sun darkened, moon no light, stars falling. These apocalyptic images describe the collapse of the present world order. The generation that sees these things begins: MacArthur takes this as the generation alive at the end times, who will not pass away before the parousia.

MacArthur: No one knows the day or the hour -- not the angels, not even the Son in his human nature. The uncertainty is not a puzzle but a summons: therefore keep watch. The parable of the faithful and wicked servants defines what watching means: not passive waiting but faithful discharge of assigned responsibilities until the master returns.

Calvin on 'be ready, because the Son of Man will come at an hour when you do not expect him' (v.44): we are to live as those who may be called at any moment. The readiness that comes from constant faithfulness is more sustainable than the preparedness that waits for warning signs to appear.

Calvin on the faithful servant: the person who continues doing their duty without calculating whether the master will be pleased or will return in time to see it is the person whose faithfulness is genuine. The wicked servant reveals his character in the absence of the master: the presence was the only thing keeping him honest.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'as lightning comes from the east and flashes to the west' discusses the contrast with the false messiahs (vv.23-26) who appear in specific places. The parousia requires no special location or insiders' knowledge: it will be as unmistakable and universal as lightning.

The NET note on 'not even the Son' (oude ho huios, v.36) discusses the significant textual variant: some manuscripts omit this phrase (perhaps from concern about the implication for Christ's omniscience). The note supports the longer reading as original: Christ's human nature did not possess all the Father's knowledge during the incarnation, in accordance with the kenotic self-limitation of Phil 2:7.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson on 'coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory' (v.30, echoing Dan 7:13): the Son of Man's coming is the climactic event the discourse has been building toward. On v.36 (no one knows the day or hour): Robertson notes this is the sharpest epistemological boundary in the discourse -- all the signs described do not amount to knowledge of the timing.

Robertson on the twin parables of readiness (the faithful and wicked servants, vv.45-51): the faithful servant is found doing his duty when the master returns. The servant who says 'my master is delayed' and begins to mistreat fellow servants reveals that the master's presence was the only thing keeping him honest. Delay does not license misconduct; readiness is active faithfulness, not passive waiting.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom: God conceals the day of judgment so that we may live every day as if it were our last. If we knew the date, we would live carelessly until near the end. The uncertainty enforces constant readiness -- which is more valuable than any calculated preparation.

The Catena traces the patristic reading of the faithful and wicked servants as a portrait of church leaders. Chrysostom: the servant put in charge of the household is the bishop or presbyter entrusted with the community's care. The temptation is the same for all: to use the master's absence as an occasion for self-service.

hist.context: The second half moves from signs to the Son of Man's unmistakable coming (vv.29-31), the fig tree lesson (vv.32-35), and the definitive unknown-timing statement (v.36). The Noah analogy is about surprise, not moral decline. The three applications (one taken, thief in the night, faithful servant) all make the same point: keep watch and do the master's work.

sections.header: Verses 29–44 — The Son of Man Coming; The Fig Tree; No One Knows

heb.paragraph: Parousia means 'presence' or 'arrival' — used for the official visit of a king or dignitary. It is not merely Jesus' return but his royal manifestation — the moment when the king who was absent becomes present, visibly and incontestably. Four times in chapter 24 (vv.3, 27, 37, 39).

'Keep watch, because you do not know on what day your Lord will come.' The verb means literally to stay awake — to resist the drowsiness of routine and complacency. Vigilance is not anxiety but attentive readiness, the posture of a servant expecting the master's return.

tl.name: Olivet Discourse — this passage
tl.text: "Not one stone here will be left on another; every one will be thrown down." Fulfilled in AD 70 when Titus destroys Jerusalem and the Temple. The specific prediction grounds the eschatological discourse in verifiable historical fulfilment.

poi.coords: East of Jerusalem; overlooking the Temple

The mountain's elevation and its view of the Temple grounds the eschatological discourse in a specific visible reality.

refs.note: The abomination of desolation -- Daniel's phrase applied to Jerusalem's coming desecration.
Gospel preached to all nations (v.14) -- connecting eschatological promise directly to the Great Commission. Mission and eschatology are inseparable.

notes.note: MacArthur: On 44 — The Son of Man Coming; The Fig Tree; No One Knows: tions." Matthew is announcing that his Gospel is a new Genesis, a new act of divine creation. This is not accidental: Matthew's Gospel is the most Jewish of the four, deliberately structured to present Jesus as the fulfilment of Israel's entire history and Scripture.

Calvin: On 44 — The Son of Man Coming; The Fig Tree; No One Knows:  as Luke does, but with the human lineage — because his purpose is to show that the Christ who came in the flesh is the very one promised to David and Abraham. The order (David before Abraham) places the Davidic covenant foremost, since it is the more specific promise concerning the Messiah's nature and office.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 44 — The Son of Man Coming; The Fig Tree; No One Knows:  of cosmic or human origins. The note flags that Matthew may intend his Gospel to be read as a "new Genesis" — the story of a new creation beginning with Jesus. The translation choice "record" is more specific than the Greek warrants; "book of origins" would preserve the allusion to Genesis more clearly.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson: On 44 — The Son of Man Coming; The Fig Tree; No One Knows: tent of it. Robertson notes this is the exact phrase used in the LXX of Gen 2:4 (the heavens and the earth) and Gen 5:1 (the generations of Adam). Matthew's opening words place his work consciously in the tradition of the Hebrew creation and genealogical accounts.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Patristic commentary on 44 — The Son of Man Coming; The Fig Tree; No One Knows: omen, and it was not necessary to mention the maternal descent, since the whole matter of the dispensation was in the legal ancestry through Joseph. By showing that Joseph is of the line of David, he shows that Mary also is, since they would not have contracted a marriage between one of David's line and one who was not."

hist.context: The Olivet Discourse (chs.24-25) is Matthew's fifth and final discourse. The first half addresses signs preceding the end: birth pains (wars, famines, persecution), the abomination, the great distress, and false messiahs. The discourse interweaves near fulfilment (Jerusalem, AD 70) and far fulfilment (the Son of Man's return) simultaneously.

sections.header: Verses 45–51 — The Faithful and Wise Servant

heb.paragraph: The good servant is both pistos (faithful, trustworthy) and phronimos (wise, shrewd). Faithfulness without wisdom is bumbling; wisdom without faithfulness is self-serving. The master's commendation requires both — reliable character combined with intelligent stewardship.

tl.name: The Son of Man's coming — this passage
tl.text: "About that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father." The discourse refuses all date-setting. The response to uncertainty is not calculation but faithfulness: keep watch, be ready.

ppl.name: The disciples
ppl.role: Ask two questions; receive one answer
ppl.text: Temple destruction and end of age answered together. Watchfulness is the conclusion.

ppl.name: The faithful servant
ppl.text: The eschatological ideal: doing the master's work regardless of when he returns.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"But about that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father."NoteThe definitive eschatological humility text. The unknown timing is a design feature -- every generation in permanent alertness.

src.title: The fall of Jerusalem, AD 70
src.quote: Josephus's Jewish War provides a harrowing account -- the abomination, the flight, the unequalled distress all have historical referents.

src.title: Two-horizon interpretation
src.quote: Most Matthew scholars favour a two-horizon reading: the discourse addresses both Jerusalem (near) and the final parousia (far).
src.note: The interpretation affects eschatology but not the practical conclusion: keep watch.

rec.text: Every generation produces date-setters who ignore 24:36. Augustine, Luther, Spurgeon all warned against it.
Carey and the modern missionary movement cited 24:14 as the theological basis for global mission.

rows.label: No Stone on Another: The Prediction That Frames the Discourse
rows.text: Not one stone here will be left on another -- every one will be thrown down. Fulfilled in AD 70. The specific historical prediction grounds the eschatological teaching that follows.

rows.label: No Stone on Another: The Prediction That Frames the Discourse
rows.text: Not one stone here will be left on another -- every one will be thrown down. Fulfilled in AD 70. The specific historical prediction grounds the eschatological teaching that follows.

rows.label: Be Ready: Faithfulness, Not Calculation — 24:42-51
rows.text: No one knows the day or hour -- not the angels, not even the Son. The uncertainty is the pedagogical point: readiness comes from constant faithfulness, not from calculated preparedness triggered by warning signs.

tx.title: This generation will not pass away
tx.content: Greek: he genea haute ou me parelthe heos an panta tauta genetai. Genea (generation) most naturally means the contemporary generation. Most preterist and many evangelical interpreters read this as pointing to AD 70.
tx.note: The AD 70 fulfilment is the near-term referent of at least part of the discourse. The end-time coming of the Son of Man is the far-term referent. The discourse's double-reference structure allows both within a single teaching.

debate.title: Does the Olivet Discourse Refer to AD 70, the Future Parousia, or Both?

positions.name: Entirely AD 70 (preterist)
positions.argument: All of vv.4-34 was fulfilled in AD 70. V.36 begins the discussion of the actual end-time parousia.

positions.name: Entirely future (dispensationalist)
positions.argument: The discourse is entirely future-oriented. The Abomination of Desolation is still future. 'This generation' means the generation alive at the end.

positions.name: Double reference: AD 70 + future parousia
positions.argument: The discourse uses the near event (AD 70) as a type and first-stage fulfilment of the far event (the parousia). Vv.4-34 have both a near and a far referent; v.36 shifts exclusively to the end-time.

scores.label: Signs of the Age: Birth Pangs

themes.note: Matthew 24 is the Olivet Discourse's first movement: Temple prediction, signs of the age, Abomination of Desolation, and the call to readiness. The chapter navigates the near event (AD 70) and the far event (the parousia) within a double-reference structure that uses the historical judgment as the pledge and type of the eschatological judgment.

coaching.tip: 'Not one stone here will be left on another' (v. 2). The disciples marvel at the temple; Jesus predicts its destruction. AD 70 looms. Eschatological discourse follows: what signs precede the end?
'About that day or hour no one knows' (v. 36). After signs and warnings, radical uncertainty: even the Son doesn't know the timing. Watchfulness, not calculation, is commanded.
